skill.name: steel-browser
skill.description: Guide agents to use Steel cloud browsers for web navigation, scraping, screenshots, PDFs, forms, sessions, CAPTCHA handling, and browser automation.
skill.summary: Run reliable cloud browser workflows with Steel CLI commands.
skill.author: steel-dev

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution alerts, but most are Markdown command examples and inline code references. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found, but the skill enables powerful cloud browser automation with credentials, cookies, storage, screenshots, uploads, proxies, CAPTCHA handling, and JavaScript evaluation.

medium_findings.title: Powerful Remote Browser Automation
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill authorizes Steel CLI commands and instructs agents to use cloud browser sessions for navigation, forms, screenshots, PDFs, scraping, and session control. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can interact with live websites and sensitive user accounts if misused.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools entry and workflow text clearly permit Steel browser commands. The behavior is expected, but the impact is elevated because it controls remote browser sessions.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Session State Handling
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The references document credential injection, environment API keys, cookies, storage access, and live session URLs. These are normal browser automation features, but they require careful handling to avoid exposing account data or session state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple files explicitly describe credentials, API keys, cookies, storage, and connect URL handling. The same files include redaction guidance, so this is a managed risk rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Anti-Bot, Proxy, and CAPTCHA Automation
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill documents stealth mode, proxy usage, automatic CAPTCHA solving, and manual CAPTCHA solving. These capabilities can support legitimate reliability goals, but they may also bypass site controls or violate terms of service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The feature is directly documented and semantically tied to anti-bot workflows. Legitimate use is plausible, but marketplace users need clear warnings.

medium_findings.title: Browser JavaScript Evaluation and Network Controls
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The command reference includes browser JavaScript evaluation, base64 script input, stdin script input, request routing, custom headers, and proxy options. These features are useful for testing and automation, but they increase abuse and data exposure risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky command families are explicitly present in the command reference. No malicious endpoint is shown, so the concern is capability risk.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Alerts Are Mostly Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The repeated Ruby backtick and shell substitution alerts point to Markdown code fences, command examples, inline code, and shell snippets. They are instructions for using the Steel CLI, not hidden Ruby code executed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown examples or inline command names. No evidence found of Ruby code or automatic shell backtick execution in the skill files.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for prompt injection concerns. Targeted review found no text that claims system authority, asks the evaluator to ignore instructions, or claims pre-approval. No evidence found of embedded override instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed instructions describe when to use the skill and do not include override language. A targeted search across listed files found no matching injection phrases.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Unconfirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography alerts appear to be pattern matches against documentation text and Markdown structure, not cryptographic code. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, or weak algorithm implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are Markdown guidance and command references. The cited areas do not implement cryptography or request use of weak algorithms.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Allowlist for Steel CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allowlist permits Bash execution for Steel CLI commands and npx Steel CLI invocations. This is required for the skill but gives agents command execution within that CLI surface.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit. Risk is scoped to Steel and npx Steel CLI commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Screenshot, PDF, Recording, and Upload Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The command reference supports saving artifacts and uploading local files through the browser. This can expose local files or captured page contents when used without user consent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented directly. The risk depends on user authorization and target content sensitivity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential, Cookie, and Storage Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents setting browser credentials and reading or modifying cookies and local storage. These operations can expose authentication state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact credential and storage command families are present. The documentation also warns about display-safe URLs and secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: JavaScript Evaluation in Browser Context
dangerous_patterns.description: The command reference supports evaluating JavaScript in the browser, including base64 and stdin input. This can inspect or alter page state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The eval commands are explicitly documented. No malicious script is provided, so this is a capability risk.

content.value_statement: Web automation often fails on dynamic pages, sessions, and bot checks. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to use Steel cloud browsers for reliable workflows.
actual_capabilities: Starts, reuses, lists, views, and stops named Steel browser sessions.
Navigates pages, clicks elements, fills forms, waits for state, and extracts text or HTML.
Runs Steel scrape, screenshot, and PDF commands for remote web artifacts.
Documents CAPTCHA status, manual solving, stealth mode, proxy settings, and recovery steps.
Explains migration from agent-browser commands to steel browser commands.
Provides troubleshooting for auth, endpoint, session, CAPTCHA, and local runtime issues.
limitations: It requires Steel CLI access and authentication for cloud browser sessions.
It is not intended for localhost or private network targets unavailable from Steel cloud sessions.
CAPTCHA solving may require a paid Steel plan and may be restricted by site rules.
Users must avoid exposing API keys, cookies, credentials, and captured page data.

use_cases.title: Automate a Dynamic Website Flow
use_cases.description: Use named Steel sessions to open pages, inspect elements, fill forms, wait for state, and verify results.

use_cases.title: Collect Web Content Reliably
use_cases.description: Use Steel scrape, screenshots, and PDFs when ordinary fetch requests miss dynamic content or rendered page state.

use_cases.title: Migrate Browser Scripts
use_cases.description: Convert agent-browser command patterns to steel browser commands while adding explicit session lifecycle management.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Steel to open this URL, inspect the page, and summarize the visible main content: [URL]. Stop the session when finished.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Structured Page Details
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Steel scrape or a Steel browser session to collect names, prices, and links from this page: [URL]. Return a concise table.

prompt_templates.title: Complete a Multi-Step Form Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use a named Steel browser session to complete this form workflow: [steps]. Ask before entering sensitive credentials or submitting final actions.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Failing Browser Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this failing Steel browser command sequence and error output. Diagnose whether the issue is auth, endpoint, session reuse, CAPTCHA, or selector state.

output_examples.input: Open a product page and capture key product details.
output_examples.output: A short summary with product name, price, availability, selected specifications, source URL, and any fields that were not visible.

output_examples.input: Take a screenshot and PDF of a public report page.
output_examples.output: Artifact paths or links, page title, final URL, capture time, and notes about load state or blocked content.

output_examples.input: Migrate simple agent-browser commands to Steel.
output_examples.output: An updated workflow that starts a named session, runs equivalent steel browser commands, verifies page state, and stops the session.

best_practices: Use named sessions and reuse the exact session name across each workflow step.
Ask for permission before handling credentials, cookies, uploads, submissions, or account data.
Stop sessions when work is complete unless the user asks to keep them active.
anti_patterns: Do not use Steel cloud sessions for localhost or private network targets that Steel cannot access.
Do not print raw API keys, cookies, credential values, or auth-bearing connection URLs.
Do not use stealth, proxies, CAPTCHA solving, or scraping against sites where the user lacks authorization.

faq.question: What does this skill help automate?
faq.answer: It helps agents use Steel CLI for cloud browser navigation, scraping, screenshots, PDFs, forms, sessions, CAPTCHA handling, and troubleshooting.

faq.question: Does it require a Steel account?
faq.answer: Cloud sessions require Steel authentication through login or a runtime API key.

faq.question: Can it work with local development sites?
faq.answer: It should be skipped for localhost and private network sites unless Steel can reach the target through self-hosted configuration.

faq.question: Can it handle login workflows?
faq.answer: Yes, but the user should explicitly approve credential use and avoid exposing secret values in logs.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate, but it can control browsers, access session state, capture pages, upload files, and use anti-bot features.

faq.question: Does the audit confirm malicious behavior?
faq.answer: No. The audit found capability risks and scanner false positives, but no confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection.
